metric_value: Covers introducing Aberdeen, conducting supplier briefings, following up prospects, and giving press interviews
notes: Four 'Critical Communication Times' define Session 2 scope

metric_name: Core objective: describe Aberdeen in under 5 minutes
metric_value: Describe Aberdeen in less than 5 minutes, without slides, to a relatively unknowledgeable audience
notes: Explicit 5-minute verbal pitch requirement; key is words and enthusiasm in voice

metric_value: Primarily held by three partners (Logan, Willmott, Kastner) and other members; private capital obtained in 1998
notes: Explicit naming of three partners as primary owners; 1998 private capital round confirmed

metric_name: Aberdeen staff count and growth rate (FAQ)
metric_value: Over 50 analysts on staff; growing approximately 50% per year
source_page: FAQ: How large is Aberdeen? slide
notes: Key metric for analyst positioning conversations

metric_value: Founders wanted a name implying IT as tool for business advantage, not domain of high priests of mainframe computing
source_page: FAQ: Where did the name Aberdeen come from?
notes: Also noted: Aberdeen/'Ab' positioning in alphabetical analyst listings

metric_value: Founded over 12 years ago (implies founding ~1988)
source_page: FAQ: How long has Aberdeen been in business?
notes: Consistent with known 1988 founding date

metric_value: Unique by strength of 1-on-1 consulting and analytical capabilities; rarely compete directly against them for a client contract
source_page: FAQ: Who are your major competitors?
notes: Aberdeen positions as complementary rather than direct competitor

metric_value: Has never used brochures; business comes from word-of-mouth; web site is living brochure
source_page: FAQ: Can I have a brochure?
notes: Policy of no brochures since founding; ~40,000 hits/month to web site mostly from users

metric_name: Aberdeen matrix research model vs. stovepiped competitors
metric_value: Multiple structured practice areas; analysts share findings at Friday staff meetings to build Aberdeen research consensus
notes: Practice areas cover: systems platforms, networking/telecom, network services/middleware, enterprise business applications, professional services

metric_value: Analysts share findings from practice areas at Friday staff meetings to build Aberdeen research consensus
notes: Cross-disciplinary consensus-building mechanism central to Aberdeen's differentiation

metric_name: Profile vs. Viewpoint vs. Impact distinction (introduction context)
metric_value: Profile: objective review of supplier go-to-market strategy; Viewpoint: analyst opinion; Impact: market-effect synopsis
notes: Three distinct publication types used to introduce Aberdeen to supplier prospects

metric_value: Target audience: supplier executives who do not yet know Aberdeen or whose knowledge comes from intermediaries (PR firms, press quotes)
notes: Same introduction can be modified for IS decision makers, press, recruits, family, plane seatmates

metric_name: Press interview style principle: vivid imagery
metric_value: This is the start of a nuclear war between IBM and DEC
notes: Exemplar quote for press sound-bite style; vivid imagery exemplar explicitly taught in training

metric_value: Trade weeklies have a noon Thursday deadline; expect calls on Wednesday; product announcements are typically on Tuesdays
notes: Analysts must respond promptly even when on the road to influence or appear in the story

metric_value: Aberdeen honors all NDAs — written or verbal; do not break the trust of others even in your enthusiasm
notes: If reporter aggressively seeks NDA information, simply discontinue conversation

metric_value: The reporters are frequently looking for Aberdeen bias — especially if your analysis differs from theirs
notes: Training explicitly addresses how reporters test for pay-for-play bias

metric_value: Never say or imply that Aberdeen will write positive comments if a supplier becomes a client — that's extortion
notes: Explicit policy statement: 'timely, objective, accurate analysis is the only way to help our clients'

metric_name: Press publications that rarely quote analysts
metric_value: Investors Business Daily does not like to quote individual analysts; Business Week quotes analysts on a relatively rare basis
notes: But both publications do want educational background from analysts

metric_value: Report the announcement; quote analyst opinion; quote customer impact; summarize editor opinion
notes: Reporter cannot write own opinion directly — reserved for editorial column

metric_value: Experienced Aberdeen analysts have been burned: positive and negative analysis, only negative printed; quotes taken out of context; confidential info printed
notes: Training warns that analyst quotes can be misrepresented or attributed to them without recollection

metric_name: Supplier briefing golden rule: analyst is in charge
metric_value: The analyst is in charge of the meeting; the supplier gave them 1-2 hours of valuable analyst time
notes: Session interactive; analyst should direct flow; challenge and support are most frequent modes

metric_name: Supplier briefing golden rule: session interactivity
metric_value: Keep the session interactive; base comments on 'Aberdeen research shows...' where possible
notes: Do not be condescending or arrogant — this is an executive-to-executive forum

metric_name: Supplier briefing: asking for three key takeaways
metric_value: Ask at any time: What are the three major points you want me to take away from this meeting?
notes: Technique to clarify supplier communication goals and redirect non-productive briefings

metric_name: Kill the Message Not the Messenger principle
metric_value: Do not assume the presenter prepared the presentation; politely but pointedly state the Aberdeen view; use company not 'you' for negative comments
notes: Presenter may be the target customer-prospect if presentation messages are incorrect

metric_value: Go to Aberdeen intranet; modify proposal; review with manager; send with copy to Aberdeen sales; follow up by phone for verbal acceptance/rejection
source_page: If Supplier Asks For Profile Proposal slide
notes: Reference to http://aberdeen2/intranet/ as proposal source

metric_value: Price at $2,000-$3,000 per person-day; never actually know how many person-days as client will change tasks and scope
source_page: If Supplier Asks For RAMP Proposal slide
notes: Candid admission about project scope volatility built into RAMP pricing guidance

metric_name: The Thin Line in the Sand principle
metric_value: There is a thin line between when suppliers are getting to know Aberdeen/analyst before acquiring services and when they are looking for free advice continuously
source_page: Thin Line in the Sand slide
notes: After 6 months of free advice: 'We need to formalize this relationship in the form of a Retainer service'

metric_value: Aberdeen described to investment community as fast growing and profitable; privately held; moving towards IPO as soon as possible
notes: Investment community audience messaging distinct from supplier, IS, or press audiences

metric_value: Try to relax them at the start by getting off the subject; talk family, hobbies, travel; say something nice; ease tension
notes: Some senior supplier executives have never worked with analysts before or have had bad experiences

metric_name: PR firm role in supplier briefings
metric_value: PR representative: gets paid to schedule meetings; gets to keep client if analyst says nice things; may act as court reporter or take active role
notes: Always treat PR representative as professional; they bring more prospective clients; may recommend purchase of Profile etc.

metric_value: Aberdeen web site gets approximately 40,000 hits per month, mostly from users
source_page: Modifications For Other Audiences: IS Decision Makers
notes: Cited to IS decision maker audience; web site has more free research than any other IT market research firm

metric_name: Reaching closure tip: pre-announce press comment
metric_value: Call prospect and say: 'The press has been asking me about your announcement and this is what I have been telling them'
notes: They will want positive analysis communicated to more people than read edited quotes

metric_value: Call prospect at 3:00 PM on Friday: 'I am planning my schedule for next month and have limited time. Do you think I should schedule start of this project?'
notes: Creates schedule urgency; prompts prospect to declare their level of interest

metric_value: Aberdeen analysts dress formally because they are also business people and consultants; overdressed better than underdressed
source_page: FAQ: Why do Aberdeen analysts dress formally?
notes: Best clients explicitly advised Aberdeen that formal dress is preferred
